slots.text: Most founders skip idea validation because they think they already know their users. Then they build for 6 months and discover they were solving the wrong problem.

Three things that kill user interviews:

1. Leading questions ("Would you use a tool that...')
2. Talking more than listening
3. Asking what they want instead of what they actually do

AI tools promise to eliminate manual work but often just move the friction somewhere else. You still need to format the output, check for errors, and integrate it into your actual workflow. The time savings evaporate in the handoffs.
Users will tell you a workflow takes "a few minutes" when it actually takes 20 minutes and involves three different apps. They've just accepted the pain as normal.
Building features users ask for feels productive. Building features that solve the problems they complain about is usually more valuable.

Signs you're validating wrong:

1. Everyone says "interesting idea"
2. Lots of feature requests, no urgency
3. People ask about pricing but don't buy
4. Users need convincing of the problem
Most founders skip talking to users because they're scared their idea sucks. But finding out your idea sucks is the point. Better to learn it costs $0 than $50k.

Three things that kill user interviews:

1. Leading questions that fish for validation
2. Talking to people who will never buy
3. Asking what they want instead of what they do

The AI tooling space feels like 2012 mobile apps. Everyone building because they can, not because users need it. Most AI features solve problems that don't exist or add complexity where simplicity worked fine.
Founders who validate ideas properly spend 60% of their time talking to users, 30% building quick tests, 10% analyzing. Most do the inverse and wonder why they build things nobody wants.
Removing friction sounds simple until you realize every team member has a different definition of what's friction and what's necessary process.

Signs your workflow has too much friction:

1. People create workarounds instead of using it
2. New hires need a week to understand it
3. You spend more time managing the process than doing the work
Most founders think user interviews are about validating their idea. They're actually about discovering which idea you should be building instead.

Why your first 10 user calls will feel useless:

1. You'll ask leading questions without realizing it
2. People will be polite instead of honest
3. You'll hear what confirms your bias
4. The real problems hide behind surface complaints

The hardest part about building with AI isn't the tech - it's figuring out where humans actually want to hand off control. Everyone says they want automation until they see what the machine chose for them.

Pattern: founders who obsess over removing friction from their own workflows end up building tools other founders want. Founders who obsess over removing friction from customer workflows build actual businesses.
Idea validation isn't a checklist you complete. It's a muscle you develop by repeatedly being wrong about what people actually want.

The workflow tools that stick aren't the ones that do everything perfectly. They're the ones that do one annoying thing well enough that you forget it used to be annoying. Everything else becomes feature creep.

Most founders validate ideas by asking "would you use this?" Wrong question. Ask "what did you do the last 3 times this problem came up?" The specific actions people already take reveal way more than hypotheticals.

User interviews that actually work:
1. Record everything (with permission)
2. Ask about past behavior, not future intent
3. Focus on one specific moment they felt the pain
4. Never mention your solution
5. End with "who else has this problem?"

Been thinking about why so many AI features feel like solutions looking for problems. Most builders start with "what can AI do?" instead of "what manual task is driving people crazy?" The magic happens when AI disappears into workflows people already have.

Pattern noticed: founders who build fast and iterate ship products people actually want. Founders who spend months perfecting before launch usually build something nobody asked for. Speed forces you to stay connected to reality.
Friction isn't always bad. Sometimes friction is the only thing standing between your user and a mistake they'll regret. The goal isn't to remove all friction—it's to remove the right friction.

Signs you're removing the wrong friction:
1. Users keep asking for confirmation dialogs you removed
2. Support tickets about accidental actions increase
3. Power users build workarounds to slow themselves down
4. "Undo" becomes your most-used feature
Most founders skip validation because they're afraid of hearing 'no.' But 'no' with reasons is infinitely more valuable than 'maybe' with silence.

3 things that kill user interviews:
1. Leading questions that confirm your bias
2. Talking more than listening
3. Asking what they want instead of what they do

Building with AI feels like having a really smart intern who occasionally hallucinates. You get 80% faster iteration but need 3x more validation to catch the weird edge cases it confidently gets wrong.
Friction isn't always bad. Sometimes the steps you want to remove are the ones that make users think twice about decisions that actually matter.
The best validation happens when potential users start asking you 'when can I use this' instead of you asking them 'would you use this.'

4 signs your workflow automation is working:
1. You forget it exists
2. New team members don't know it wasn't always there
3. Breaking it causes immediate panic
4. You can't remember the manual process
The hardest part of idea validation isn't getting users to talk. It's getting them to tell you the truth instead of what they think you want to hear.

Three things that kill user interviews:

1. Leading questions that telegraph the answer you want
2. Talking about your solution instead of their problem
3. Taking politeness for genuine interest
When someone says your idea is "interesting" and then changes the subject, that's not validation. That's social lubrication.

The AI tooling paradox: we're building AI to remove friction from workflows, but adding AI often creates new friction points. The integration overhead, the prompt engineering, the hallucination checking. Sometimes the cure is worse than the disease.
User feedback follows a power law. 80% is noise, 15% is useful, 5% fundamentally changes how you think about the problem.

Red flags when validating an AI product idea:

1. The human process works fine already
2. You need perfect accuracy but AI gives you 85%
3. The workflow has 12 steps and you're only solving step 3
4. Compliance risk outweighs efficiency gains
Most founders skip validation because they're afraid of hearing 'no.' But 'no' with reasons is infinitely more valuable than 'yes' from people being polite.

User interviews that actually matter:
1. Ask about past behavior, not future intentions
2. Listen for what they built themselves to solve it
3. Notice when they pay money vs just complain
4. Watch for workarounds they've already created

Everyone talks about 'removing friction' but most founders add it back by over-engineering. Built a simple workflow tool, then spent weeks adding features nobody asked for. Simple wins, but simple is hard.
Founders who validate well ask 'What did you try before this?' Bad validators ask 'Would you use this?' The difference is past tense vs future tense. Past behavior predicts future behavior.
AI makes it tempting to build everything. But the real skill is knowing what not to build. Constraint forces clarity.

Signs your validation is working:
1. People interrupt you to ask when it's ready
2. They offer to pay before you mention pricing
3. They introduce you to others with the same problem
4. They explain your solution better than you do
